steps.title: Track the passage structure
steps.content: The text introduces The Hundred Days as part of Patrick O'Brian's Aubrey/Maturin series. It then describes a structural feature that some critics object to: abrupt endings that seem arbitrary rather than conclusive. After that, the text gives a contrasting viewpoint from other critics.

steps.title: Choose the two-view summary
steps.content: The second viewpoint says the books should be understood not as separate novels with traditional beginnings and endings, but as one very long work, like other multivolume stories. Choice A invents a claim about complexity. Choice B turns the Proust comparison into a claim about renown. Choice D adds entertainment value despite structural flaws, which the text never discusses. The answer is C.
